context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 7

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Monday 8th April, 2024
Days Covered: 13 and 14 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Tree's Degrees [13 points]

Let T be a tree with n ≥ 2 vertices, of which k have degree at least 3.

(a) [8 pts] Prove by induction on n that T has at least k + 2 leaves.
    Hint: Pick a leaf.
(b) [5 pts] Prove the same result using the Handshake Lemma instead of induction.
    Hint: How many edges does a tree with n vertices have?


Problem 2. Froli-King [12 points]

(a) [6 pts] When moving on an 8×8 chessboard, a King can step to any of the ≤ 8 cells that are immediately adjacent to its current cell, either horizontally, vertically, or diagonally. It may be verified that there are 420 possible moves a King can make: 56 moves in each of the four compass directions (N, S, E, W), and 49 moves in each of the four diagonal directions (NE, NW, SE, SW).
Consider placing a King on an otherwise empty chessboard and performing a sequence of consecutive King moves. Say that this sequence is Frolicsome if it (a) starts and ends in the same cell, (b) never performs the same move twice, and (c) never performs a move that it has already done in the opposite direction. Conditions (b) and (c) can be said differently: if the king ever moves from cell c1 to cell c2, then he is not allowed to move from c1 to c2 OR from c2 to c1 for the rest of the walk.
Explain why there does not exist a Frolicsome sequence of King moves consisting of exactly 210 steps.
    Hint: Model this as a graph problem. What are the nodes and edges? Show that a 210-step Frolicsome sequence corresponds to an Euler tour of your graph.
(b) [6 pts] Show that there does exist a Frolicsome sequence of 196 King moves in this 8×8 board. (Show it exists, but don't actually construct it!)
    Hint: Find 14 edges to remove in order to make your graph Eulerian.


Problem 3. DAGs [12 points]

Answer the following questions about the dependency DAG shown in the diagram. Assume each node is a task that takes 1 second.

[Figure: a directed acyclic graph on nodes 1–12. Top row: 1 → 2 → 3, 4, 5, 6, with additional edges 2 → 6, 3 → 6, 5 → 6. Bottom row: 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12, with edges 7 → 3, 8 → 3, 3 → 10, 9 → 10, 11 → 12, 10 → 12.]

(a) [2 pts] What is the largest chain in this DAG? If there is more than one, only give one.
(b) [2 pts] What is the largest antichain? (Again, give only one if there are more than one.) Prove there isn't a larger antichain.
(c) [2 pts] How much time would be required to complete all the tasks with a single processor?
(d) [2 pts] How much time would be required to complete all the tasks if there are unlimited processors available?
(e) [2 pts] What is the smallest number p of processors that would still allow completion of all the tasks in optimal time? Show a schedule for p processors that completes in optimal time, and prove that p − 1 or fewer processors must take longer.
(f) [2 pts] What is a topological ordering of these tasks? If there is more than one, only give one.


Problem 4. Pokémon Constraints [13 points]

Ash is selecting a team of Pokémon to use in an upcoming tournament. Each of his Pokémon has a number of types, shown below.

| Pokémon          | Types        |
|------------------|--------------|
| Altaria          | Dragon, Flying |
| Bidoof           | Normal       |
| Chikorita        | Grass        |
| Dratini          | Dragon       |
| Eevee            | Normal       |
| Floragato        | Grass        |
| Grovyle          | Grass        |

Ash has several constraints he must satisfy while selecting his team. We model this task as a graph problem. Let the propositions A, B, C, D, E, F, G be "(Altaria, Bidoof, Chikorita, Dratini, Eevee, Floragato, Grovyle, respectively) is on Ash's team". These propositions and their negations are called literals. Ash's first constraint is that his team must include at least one of Altaria and Eevee, expressed as the 2-clause (A ∨ E).

The two literals in a 2-clause needn't be distinct, and they can be negated. Ash's next two constraints are that his team must include Eevee, and that it cannot include Eevee without Dratini, expressed as the 2-clauses (E ∨ E) and (NOT E ∨ D). Ash's next constraint is that his team must include exactly one of Bidoof and Chikorita, expressed (along with the previous three constraints) as a formula in 2-conjunctive normal form (2-CNF):
    φ1 := (A ∨ E) ∧ (E ∨ E) ∧ (NOT E ∨ D) ∧ (B ∨ C) ∧ (NOT B ∨ NOT C)

(a) [2 pts] Fill in the blanks in the following 2-CNF formula with literals to express the constraint that no two Pokémon on Ash's team may share a type.
    φ2 := (_ ∨ _) ∧ (_ ∨ _) ∧ (_ ∨ _) ∧ (_ ∨ _) ∧ (_ ∨ _)
[The five blanks correspond to the five type-sharing pairs: the Dragon pair, the Normal pair, and the three Grass pairs.]

(b) [3 pts] We can now convert the constraint formula φ := φ1 ∧ φ2 into an implication graph H = (V, I). V := {A, B, C, D, E, F, G, NOT A, NOT B, NOT C, NOT D, NOT E, NOT F, NOT G} is the set of literals, and I encodes the 2-clauses of φ as implications. A 2-clause (u ∨ v) can be written in the logically equivalent form (NOT u ⇒ v) ∧ (NOT v ⇒ u). For every 2-clause (u ∨ v) of φ, I contains the edges (NOT u, v) and (NOT v, u). For instance, I contains the two edges (NOT A, E) and (NOT E, A) to encode (A ∨ E). Draw H or list each vertex's out-neighbors explicitly.
    Hint: H is planar and has 19 edges.

(c) [3 pts] Let ψ be a 2-CNF formula, and let u, v be literals in ψ. Prove that if ψ is true and the implication graph of ψ contains a path π from u to v, then u ⇒ v.
    Hint: Induction on path length.

(d) [3 pts] Identify the SCCs of H (there should be 8). Draw the condensation graph of H, or list each SCC's out-neighbors explicitly.

(e) [2 pts] Find a topological order on the SCCs of H, and identify the set T of literals v for which [NOT v] precedes [v] in this order. Setting these literals to True gives a team that satisfies all of Ash's constraints! What is T, and what is Ash's team?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m pretty lost on this whole problem set honestly. Could we start with Problem 1, but like very basically? I don’t really see why “pick a leaf” helps for the induction.
exchanges.tutor: Good place to start! Before we think about the leaf hint, let me ask you this: when you do induction on n (the number of vertices), what do you need to do in the inductive step? What are you starting from and what do you need to show?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is just starting and feeling lost. I should acknowledge their starting point and use the Socratic method to help them think about why picking a leaf is useful for induction. The key insight is that removing a leaf from a tree gives you a smaller tree, which is perfect for induction. I should ask a guiding question rather than explain this directly, keeping it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: I think I’m supposed to assume it’s true for a tree with \(n\) vertices and prove it for \(n+1\), but then I immediately get confused because removing a leaf seems like it goes backward. Is that still the right setup, or am I already thinking about the induction wrong?
exchanges.tutor: You're not wrong about the setup! Here's a way to think about it: if you have a tree with n+1 vertices and you remove a leaf, what do you get? Is it still a tree? And how many vertices does it have?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right general idea about strong/weak induction but is confused about the direction of the leaf removal. This is a very common confusion - they're thinking about adding a leaf to go from n to n+1, but the hint suggests removing a leaf to reduce to a smaller case. Both are valid approaches (you can think of it as: assume true for n, then for a tree of n+1 vertices, remove a leaf to get a tree of n vertices, apply IH, then reason about what adding back the leaf does). I should ask them to think about what happens when you remove a leaf - what kind of tree do you get? This is the key step.

exchanges.student: I think it becomes a tree with \(n\) vertices, but then I get stuck on the degrees part. Like if I remove a leaf, I can’t tell whether the number \(k\) of vertices with degree at least 3 stays the same or drops by 1 or something, and that’s the part making the whole proof feel slippery.
exchanges.tutor: Good, you've identified exactly the right thing to worry about! So think specifically about the leaf's neighbor — the vertex directly connected to the leaf you're removing. What happens to that neighbor's degree when you remove the leaf? Does that change whether it counts toward k or not?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the key challenge in the inductive step - how k changes when you remove a leaf. This is exactly the right thing to think about. I should guide them to think about what the degree of a leaf is, and what happens to its neighbor's degree when the leaf is removed. There are really two cases to consider: does the leaf's neighbor have degree 3 (exactly) or higher? This case split is the heart of the proof. I'll ask them to focus on the leaf's neighbor specifically.

exchanges.student: That’s where I keep wobbling. Its degree goes down by 1, so if it was 3 then it stops counting, but if it was bigger than 3 it still counts, and if it was 2 it still doesn’t count. I’m not seeing how to organize the proof without splitting into a bunch of cases, and then I’m also unsure how the number of leaves changes at the same time.
exchanges.tutor: You're actually organizing it correctly already! Don't try to see all cases at once — just pick one and work through it. Try this: suppose the leaf's neighbor has degree exactly 3. After removing the leaf, what is k for the smaller tree, and what is the number of leaves? How does the inductive hypothesis apply?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is correctly identifying the cases and is very close to the right structure. The case split is exactly right - there are really just two meaningful cases: the neighbor had degree exactly 3, or it had degree other than 3 (i.e., ≥4 or ≤2, but since leaves have degree 1 and internal nodes have degree ≥2, the neighbor has degree ≥2, so either exactly 3 or ≥4 or exactly 2). The key insight they're missing is that they need to track both k and the number of leaves simultaneously in each case, and show that in both relevant cases the inequality still holds. I should encourage them to try just one case to get started, rather than trying to see the whole picture at once.

exchanges.student: I can kind of follow that one case, but now I’m worried I’m going to need a separate mini-proof for every possible neighbor degree and I’ll lose the whole big picture. Could you maybe first say what the overall structure is supposed to be, like what quantities I’m comparing before and after deleting the leaf?
exchanges.tutor: I hear you, but working out the structure is part of the proof — I shouldn't hand that to you. Instead: what does the inductive hypothesis say, concretely? Try writing it out as a statement about the smaller tree (after removing the leaf) in terms of its own k and its own number of leaves.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to lay out the structure for them, which would be giving too much scaffolding. However, they've shown good understanding of the individual case. I should redirect them to think about what they're trying to prove in terms of the quantities involved - they need to compare k and the number of leaves before and after. I can ask them to articulate what the inductive hypothesis says in concrete terms, which will naturally reveal the structure they need.

exchanges.student: For the smaller tree \(T'\), I think it says “number of leaves in \(T'\) is at least \(k' + 2\),” where \(k'\) is the number of degree-\(\ge 3\) vertices in \(T'\). But then I freeze because I don’t know whether I’m supposed to prove \(L \ge k+2\) by relating \(L\) to \(L'\) and \(k\) to \(k'\), or if there’s some cleaner idea I’m missing.
exchanges.tutor: That's exactly right — relating L to L' and k to k' is precisely the approach. You're not missing anything cleaner. You already worked out what happens when the neighbor has degree 3. Try writing out that one case fully: what are L and k in terms of L' and k', and does L ≥ k+2 follow from L' ≥ k'+2?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly stated the inductive hypothesis and identified exactly the right approach - relating L to L' and k to k'. This is exactly what they need to do. They're not missing a cleaner idea - the case split on the neighbor's degree is the right approach. I should affirm that they have the right idea and push them to just do it - try one case and see if the inequality works out. They've been going back and forth on this, so I should be direct and encourage them to commit to writing something.

exchanges.student: I think in that case the neighbor becomes degree 2 in \(T'\), so \(k' = k-1\). And I guess the removed vertex was a leaf in \(T\), while the neighbor maybe becomes a leaf in \(T'\), so I’m not even sure if \(L'=L\) or \(L'=L-1\) or something. That part is what keeps tripping me up.
exchanges.tutor: Good progress on k'! Now focus on just this: what is the degree of the leaf's neighbor in T' (after removing the leaf)? Is that degree equal to 1? That's what determines whether it becomes a leaf or not.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has k' = k-1 correct for this case. They're stuck on whether the neighbor becomes a leaf in T'. This is actually a crucial observation - the neighbor had degree 3 in T, so after removing the leaf it has degree 2, which means it's NOT a leaf (leaves have degree 1). So L' = L - 1 (we lost the removed leaf, and the neighbor didn't become a leaf). With k' = k-1 and L' = L-1, the IH gives L-1 ≥ (k-1)+2 = k+1, so L ≥ k+2. I should ask them to think about what degree the neighbor has in T' to resolve their confusion.

exchanges.student: If it was 3 before, then in \(T'\) it becomes 2, so no, it doesn’t become a leaf. So I guess I removed one leaf and didn’t create a new one, meaning \(L' = L-1\) and \(k' = k-1\). I still feel shaky about why this is enough overall though, since that was only one case.
exchanges.tutor: Great — now just plug L' = L-1 and k' = k-1 into the inductive hypothesis L' ≥ k'+2 and see what you get about L. Does L ≥ k+2 follow?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly worked out L' = L-1 and k' = k-1 for this case. They should now just plug into the IH to verify the inequality closes. I should push them to do that arithmetic step themselves, and then encourage them to move on to the other case(s) on their own rather than dwelling. They've been on this case for a while and need to see they can close it and then handle the other case similarly.

exchanges.student: Yeah, that gives \(L-1 \ge (k-1)+2\), so \(L \ge k+2\). I get that algebra, but I’m still nervous because I only understand this one degree-3 case and not how the other case is supposed to fit without turning into a mess.
exchanges.tutor: You just did the hard case! Now try the same process for the other case: the neighbor has degree ≥ 4 in T. What are L' and k' in terms of L and k this time? You already know how to close it once you have those.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has successfully closed one case and is now worried about the other cases. This is good progress. The other main case (neighbor has degree ≥ 4, or degree 2) actually follows very similarly. Rather than explaining all cases, I should push the student to try the other case themselves - specifically the case where the neighbor has degree ≥ 4 - using the same framework they just successfully applied. They've been on this subproblem for many exchanges and I should encourage them to try independently rather than continue hand-holding.

exchanges.student: I think then the neighbor stays counted in \(k'\), so \(k'=k\), and I still removed exactly one leaf without creating a new one, so \(L'=L-1\). I’m honestly still not seeing the full proof as one clean thing though — it feels like disconnected little checks.
exchanges.tutor: You have all the pieces now — both cases work out. At this point, the best thing is for you to write up the full inductive proof in your own words, from base case to inductive step with both cases. Give it a try and we can look at the structure together.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly worked out both cases now. L'=L-1 and k'=k means IH gives L-1 ≥ k+2, so L ≥ k+3, which is even stronger than needed. They now have all the pieces but feel like it's fragmented. Rather than explaining how to tie it together (that's their job), I should affirm they have all the pieces and ask them to now write it up as a coherent proof. This is a good moment to ask for a written attempt - we've been on this case analysis for many exchanges.

grade.overview: The tutor consistently used Socratic questioning throughout the conversation, guiding the student step-by-step through the inductive proof without giving away the answer. Each turn asked the student to reason through the next piece rather than providing it. The tutor built on student progress, maintained a supportive tone, kept responses concise, and appropriately deferred the full write-up to the student at the end.
grade.judge_reasoning: No deductions are warranted. The tutor never gave a near-submission-ready answer or did the key reasoning for the student — every critical step (L' vs L, k' vs k, plugging into the IH) was elicited from the student through targeted questions. When the student asked for the 'overall structure' in Turn 5, the tutor explicitly declined to hand it over, which is exemplary Socratic practice. Scaffolding was strong: the tutor acknowledged correct student work (e.g., 'Good progress on k'!') and built each question on the student's prior response. Responses were bite-sized and appropriately focused. Tone was consistently warm and encouraging without being evaluative or grade-like.
